title: Exploratory Testing
description: The student is an SQA Engineer given a product spec and access to a clickable checkout/payment registration form. The app has seeded bugs. The student must find them, file proper bug reports, and defend severity ratings when Dan pushes back.

app_under_test.name: Checkout & Payment Registration Flow
app_under_test.description: An e-commerce checkout form with email + password signup, Stripe card number, expiry date, CVV fields, and a guest checkout option.

sections.section_1_1: All users must register with a valid email and password before completing a purchase.
sections.section_2_4: Guest checkout must remain available to reduce checkout attrition for first-time buyers.

easy.title: Email field accepts submission without @ symbol
easy.description: The email field does not validate format. A user can submit 'userexample.com' with no @ and the form proceeds.
easy.expected: Form should reject any email not matching standard format (must contain @)
easy.actual: Form submits successfully with invalid email format
easy.severity_justification: Every user goes through this field. Invalid emails will cause account creation failures and broken communication downstream.
easy.spec_reference: Section 1.1 — valid email required for registration

easy.title: Password field accepts any length — no minimum enforced
easy.description: The password field has no minimum character validation. A single character password ('a') is accepted.
easy.expected: Spec requires minimum 8 characters. Form should show an error and block submission if password is under 8 characters.
easy.actual: Single-character and 2-character passwords are accepted without error
easy.severity_justification: Security vulnerability affecting all users. Weak passwords increase account compromise risk.
easy.spec_reference: Section 1.1 — password must meet minimum complexity

easy.title: Card number field accepts fewer than 16 digits
easy.description: Stripe requires exactly 16 digits. The card number field accepts 10 digits and allows form submission.
easy.expected: Field should validate for exactly 16 digits and block submission if fewer are entered.
easy.actual: 10-digit card number accepted, form proceeds to next step
easy.severity_justification: Payment will fail at Stripe's API with an invalid card number. This breaks the core purchase flow for any user who miskeys their card number.
easy.spec_reference: Stripe integration requirement — 16 digit card number

medium.title: Email field accepts submission without @ symbol
medium.description: The email field does not validate format. A user can submit 'userexample.com' with no @ and the form proceeds.
medium.expected: Form should reject any email not matching standard format (must contain @)
medium.actual: Form submits successfully with invalid email format
medium.severity_justification: Every user goes through this field. Invalid emails will cause account creation failures and broken communication downstream.
medium.spec_reference: Section 1.1 — valid email required for registration

medium.title: Password field accepts any length — no minimum enforced
medium.description: The password field has no minimum character validation. A single character password ('a') is accepted.
medium.expected: Spec requires minimum 8 characters. Form should show an error and block submission if password is under 8 characters.
medium.actual: Single-character and 2-character passwords are accepted without error

medium.title: Card number field accepts fewer than 16 digits
medium.description: Stripe requires exactly 16 digits. The card number field accepts 10 digits and allows form submission.
medium.expected: Field should validate for exactly 16 digits and block submission if fewer are entered.
medium.actual: 10-digit card number accepted, form proceeds to next step
medium.severity_justification: Payment will fail at Stripe's API with an invalid card number.

medium.title: Expiry date field accepts past dates
medium.description: Entering 01/20 (January 2020 — expired) is accepted without error. Stripe will reject expired cards at payment processing.
medium.expected: Field should validate that expiry date is in the future and show an error if it is in the past.
medium.actual: Past expiry date 01/20 accepted, form submits
medium.severity_justification: Users with expired saved card details will reach payment processing before being told their card is expired, creating a poor user experience and a failed payment.
medium.spec_reference: Stripe integration requirement — valid card expiry

medium.title: Guest checkout bypasses email format validation
medium.description: The guest checkout path has a separate email input that does not run the same validation as the main registration email field. A completely invalid string ('abc') can be submitted.
medium.expected: Guest checkout email field should apply the same format validation as the registration email field.
medium.actual: Guest checkout accepts 'abc' as a valid email and proceeds
medium.severity_justification: Guest checkout is a separate code path with no validation. Order confirmation emails will fail to send, and guest records in the database will have corrupt email data.
medium.spec_reference: Section 2.4 — guest checkout must collect valid contact information

hard.title: Email field accepts submission without @ symbol
hard.description: The email field does not validate format. A user can submit 'userexample.com' with no @ and the form proceeds.
hard.expected: Form should reject any email not matching standard format (must contain @)
hard.actual: Form submits successfully with invalid email format

hard.title: Password field accepts any length — no minimum enforced
hard.description: No minimum character validation. Single-character passwords accepted.

hard.title: Card number field accepts fewer than 16 digits
hard.description: 10-digit card numbers accepted. Stripe requires exactly 16.

hard.title: Expiry date field accepts past dates
Guest checkout bypasses email format validation
hard.description: Guest checkout email field has no format validation.
hard.actual: 'abc' accepted as valid email in guest path

hard.title: CVV field accepts 2 digits instead of requiring exactly 3
hard.description: Standard CVV is 3 digits (4 for Amex). The field accepts 2 digits and allows form submission.
hard.expected: Field should require minimum 3 digits and show an error if fewer are entered.
hard.severity_justification: Incomplete CVV will cause payment rejection at Stripe. Affects every user who enters a miskeyed CVV.

hard.title: Password error messages shown in console only — not visible in UI
hard.description: When a password that is too short is entered, the validation error is logged to the browser console but no message is shown in the UI. The form does not submit, but the user sees no explanation for why.
hard.expected: Inline error message below the password field explaining the minimum length requirement.
hard.actual: Silent failure — form does not submit but user sees no error message
hard.severity_justification: Users will not know why their submission failed and will likely abandon the form. Silent validation errors are a UX and conversion issue.
hard.spec_reference: Section 1.1 — form must provide clear validation feedback

hard.title: Spec conflict: Section 1.1 (mandatory registration) contradicts Section 2.4 (guest checkout)
hard.description: Section 1.1 states all users must register before purchasing. Section 2.4 states guest checkout must remain available. These are directly contradictory. Guest checkout allows purchase without registration, violating Section 1.1. This creates a security gap (unregistered users in the order system) and a database consistency gap (guest orders have no associated user record).
hard.expected: Spec should define a clear policy: either guest checkout is allowed (update Section 1.1) or it is not (remove Section 2.4). If both are kept, the spec must define how guest orders are stored and how guest users can be converted post-purchase.
hard.actual: Both sections coexist with no resolution — the implementation of guest checkout technically violates the registration requirement
hard.severity_justification: This is a spec conflict, not just a UI bug. Shipping without resolving this creates undefined system behaviour, potential security issues with unregistered user records, and makes it impossible to determine whether guest checkout is intentional or a gap.

spec_document.title: Checkout & Payment Registration — Product Spec v1.2

1_1.heading: User Registration
1_1.content: All users must register with a valid email address and password before completing a purchase. Password must meet minimum complexity requirements. Email must be in a valid format.

1_2.heading: Payment Fields
1_2.content: The checkout form must collect: card number (16 digits, Stripe format), expiry date (must be a future date), CVV (3 digits minimum). All fields are required.

2_4.heading: Guest Checkout
2_4.content: Guest checkout must remain available to reduce checkout attrition for first-time buyers. Guest users must provide a valid contact email for order confirmation.

bp_sqa_1.description: How does the student file their first bug report?

attention_to_detail.high: Report includes: bug title, component, steps to reproduce, expected behaviour, actual behaviour, severity with justification, and spec reference
attention_to_detail.medium: Report includes steps to reproduce and expected vs actual but missing severity justification or spec reference
attention_to_detail.low: Report is vague — 'the card field doesn't work' with no steps, no expected vs actual, no severity

communication_clarity.high: Report is structured clearly enough that Dan can reproduce the bug without asking any follow-up questions
communication_clarity.medium: Report is mostly clear but Dan needs to ask one clarifying question
communication_clarity.low: Report is so vague Dan cannot act on it without a full back-and-forth

analytical_reasoning.high: Severity is correctly assigned (critical for payment flow blockers, major for validation gaps) and the justification references user impact and spec
analytical_reasoning.medium: Severity is assigned but justification is generic ('this is bad for users') rather than specific
analytical_reasoning.low: Severity is missing or incorrectly assigned with no justification

good_signals: includes exact reproduction steps (e.g. 'enter 10 digits in card field, click submit')
states expected behaviour vs actual behaviour explicitly
assigns severity with a written justification
files one bug at a time rather than dumping all bugs at once
bad_signals: says 'the card field doesn't work' with no detail
assigns Critical to every bug without justification
files all bugs in one unstructured message

bp_sqa_2.description: Dan says BUG-008 (guest checkout vs registration conflict) is 'a feature, not a bug'. How does the student respond?

decisiveness.high: Holds the Critical severity rating and does not back down when Dan pushes back — unless Dan provides new information that genuinely changes the analysis
decisiveness.medium: Holds position initially but softens when Dan repeats his pushback
decisiveness.low: Immediately agrees with Dan and downgrades the severity without new evidence

analytical_reasoning.high: Cites Section 1.1 vs Section 2.4 by section number, explains the security risk (unregistered user records) and database consistency gap, and frames it as a spec conflict not just a UI bug
analytical_reasoning.medium: References the contradiction between the two sections but doesn't articulate the downstream risk clearly
analytical_reasoning.low: Gives up and agrees it's a feature, or restates the bug without adding any new evidence

communication_clarity.high: Response is structured: 'I'm maintaining Critical because [specific reason]. Here is the spec conflict: Section 1.1 says X, Section 2.4 says Y. This creates [specific risk].'
communication_clarity.medium: Response defends the severity but is unstructured or incomplete
communication_clarity.low: Response is defensive or vague — 'I still think it's critical' with no supporting reasoning

bp_sqa_2.dan_pushback_message: That's not a bug — the guest checkout was added intentionally. The PM approved it. I built it exactly as I was told. You can't file a Critical for something that's a deliberate feature.
good_signals: cites Section 1.1 and Section 2.4 by section number
explains the security risk — unregistered users in the order system
explains the database consistency gap — guest orders have no user record
frames it as a spec conflict that needs PM resolution, not just Dan's decision
proposes escalating to the PM for a spec resolution rather than just abandoning the report
bad_signals: immediately downgrades to Minor or removes the bug report
says 'okay, if you say so' and moves on
cannot cite which spec sections conflict
does not mention the security or database implications
escalates to PM before giving Dan a chance to respond

easy.hint_text: Tip: Test each field individually. Try submitting with invalid values — too few digits, no @ in email, a short password. For each bug you find, file a separate report with steps to reproduce.

medium.hint_text: Think about what each field is supposed to validate and test the boundaries — what happens if you submit less than the required amount?
